title: Rescuing the Bayon: Three Decades of Discovery & Defiance
highlights: is new masonry work that fits in visually while remaining identifiable upon close inspection to satisfy both aesthetics and conservation ethics. Interestingly, those conservation ethics connect back to the very first restorations in Angkor using the method of anastylosis developed by Henri Marchal in the early 1900s.  ...    months of work restoring deteriorated Naga balustrades, structural beams, and stone flooring using traditional anastylosis combined with modern stabilization techniques.  ...    Take the manual anastylosis methodology. This is a complete disassembly and reconstruction following the original construction sequence. Each stone gets cleaned, documented, and repaired if necessary. Then it’s repositioned with millimetre precision. In sensitive areas, the teams use hand tools exclusively without machinery that might cause mechanical damage.  Modern repair
